text: Hi, and welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Andy, and I'm here with Jacob from Indigo Carbon.
Hi, Jacob.
Hey, Andy.
So, Jacob, can you tell me about Indigo Carbon?
Yeah. Indigo is working with individual farmers to help them transition to regenerative agriculture,
which is a system of practices to kind of enrich their soil and get paid to do it by people that
are emitting CO2 and want to buy offsets for that.
So, how do you actually help farmers with this architecture?
Yeah. So, there's a lot of different data that needs to be collected in order to actually
produce carbon credits. We're able to leverage existing satellites from organizations like
NASA to infer a lot of things about the operation on an individual field. It all kind of starts
there. Every day throughout the day, we get a series of tiles from them that are kind of
modestly processed and corrected for a bunch of things. And we get them in, but we need
to do some post-processing both to correct some more and to get it into different formats
that are more accessible for stuff downstream. So, we spin up a big kind of cluster of spot
instances as quickly as possible, process all this data as it comes in, and spin it back
down. We store those outputs in a combination of S3 and RDS. RDS has the metadata in it, and
S3 actually has like the big bulk data itself.
Can you give me a sense of how much data we're actually talking about here?
Yeah. So, our catalog spans back actually like 30 years. Most of our data is kind of in like
the 10-year time frame. All of that surprises about five petabytes or so, give or take. And
every day that grows as we get new imagery in. And that's like 100 gigabytes a day. And
that rate increases as we add more data sources that we're doing kind of continuously.
Wow. So, after you've ingested this data, what happens next?
Yeah. So, we access it in two kind of pretty different ways. One is from direct kind of
like user access, and the other is to drive stuff in our models that we ultimately use
to generate the carbon credits. We can talk about the user flow first. When they log into
our app, one of the first things we show is this map of the United States, and we show some
imagery on top of that. And we want it to be like our most up-to-date imagery. We give them
the option to select what kind of layer they want from here and which time frame they're talking
about. But ultimately, it gets served up as web tiles that come from this Fargate cluster.
When you load a map, it gets sliced into 16 or so different tiles, and those come as individual
requests to Fargate. Fargate then looks up in RDS which source imagery actually corresponds to that
tile. And then it pulls that data from S3. S3 has relatively large compressed chunks of data. And we only need
like a small subsection of that for each one of these like source tiles. We actually use one of the kind of
main features in S3 that's useful is HTTP range requests. We can read a part of an object in S3 instead of
reading the entire thing. We then take all those different little parts of images, and we stitch them back
together into a kind of bigger image. We down sample it to the right resolution, and then we'd either do color
correction if we're talking about an RGB image, or we do color mapping if we're talking about just like
an intensity value. So if you want to show like greenness of your field, that'll be like a zero to
one value. We need to map that into like an RGB space. We have a color map for that. And then we serve
it back as a tile. So I'm seeing a few other components on here. How does ElastiCache fit into this solution?
Yeah, it's pretty simple. We just use Redis in ElastiCache as a kind of central cache for the tiles we've
already generated. A lot of the tiles in our system are pretty common. Like I said, when we first load
the app for most users, it shows the entire US. Those tiles are seen by everybody. So instead of
recomputing those on the fly every time, we compute them on the fly once and store them in ElastiCache here,
and read and write from that as needed, and serve back tiles to the user. Okay, excellent. And then so
there's one last one other component to this. I'm seeing a lambda over here. Can you tell me what this
lambda is actually doing? Yeah, so this is actually a series of lambda functions in here. What is ultimately
there to do is try to compute a time series of data from this kind of source data cube. Taking back to
the example of like the average greenness on your field, over the course of a year, say it's like 2019,
and that goes to 2020, for corn, somewhere in August is kind of your peak greenness, and it'll end up
looking in a curve like this. And this can tell you things about like the health of your corn and that
kind of thing. You can imagine there's a lot more complex stuff you can do with it. But in order to
compute that from this raw imagery, we need to take the area that we're interested in. In this case,
it's something that a farmer told us, which is like their field somewhere in there in the grid of
Iowa. And there's a little cutout here. So this is custom, this is what the grower is actually farming.
So we don't know this ahead of time. So they tell us this, we only need to take it, pass it to this
series of lambda functions. And what this does is pretty similar to what the Fargate service does here.
Initially, it pulls the imagery that it needs from by looking it up in RDS and then reading stuff from
S3. It turns that into kind of the tile of interest that we're doing. And then we clip it to this area
of interest, and then can be a summary on it. In this case, we're talking about like the mean of the
greenness. That's what it would do, take the average of all the pixels that fall in that field. And then it
does that 1000 times over to get that time series. I'm seeing a lot of serverless components on here. Why'd you
choose to go with that for this particular architecture? Yeah, so we have we have thousands
of input variables here, and all kinds of different locations in the country. We just talked about how
like we don't actually know the area that we're really interested in until the farmer tells us.
And we have time as well as another component of this. So there's millions of combinations that we
could cut this data cube. We get it to a point where it's kind of minimally processed and to do all the
other processing on the fly. And it saves us orders of magnitude of cost. Excellent. Jacob, thank you very
much for sharing this. It's great to hear how Indigo Carbon is using a largely serverless architecture to help
individual farmers with regenerative agriculture. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
